title: Decoding Back to Full Resolution
concept: After the reverse diffusion process finishes in latent space, a decoder expands the final latent code back into a full-resolution image.

exercises.prompt: When you unzip a compressed photo file on your computer, what happens?

options.text: The tiny file expands back into the full-size picture

exercises.explain: Unzipping expands a small, compact file back into its full, detailed version — just like a decoder expands a latent code into a full image!

cards.title: The Final Magic Trick
cards.body: The AI has been secretly cleaning up noise in a tiny, compressed 'sketch' space. But you can't see that sketch — it's too small and blurry to make sense of. So one last step has to happen: turning that tiny sketch into the big, beautiful, full-size picture you actually see!

cards.title: What Is Decoding?
cards.body: Decoding is the final step where a special network called a decoder takes the finished, cleaned-up latent code (the small compressed version) and expands it back into a full-resolution image — with all the sharp details, colors, and pixels restored.

cards.title: Like a Seed Growing Into a Tree
cards.body: A tiny seed doesn't look like a tree at all, but it holds all the information needed to grow one. The decoder is like sunlight and water — it takes that small 'seed' (latent code) and grows it into a full, detailed tree (the final image).

cards.title: From Sketch to Poster
cards.body: Imagine the reverse diffusion process finishes and leaves behind a small 64x64 latent grid of numbers. The decoder takes that grid and stretches, sharpens, and fills it in until it becomes a crisp 512x512 image of a dragon flying over mountains — ready to show you!

cards.title: Myth: The Latent Code IS the Picture
cards.body: Nope! The latent code is just a compressed set of numbers — it's not something you could recognize as a picture if you looked at it directly. It only becomes a real, viewable image after the decoder expands and translates it.

cards.title: Latent Code vs. Final Image
cards.body: The decoder is the bridge between these two very different forms of the same content.

left.text: Small grid of compressed numbers, not viewable as a picture, fast to process

right.text: Large, full-resolution picture with clear colors, shapes, and details you can see

exercises.prompt: What does the decoder do at the end of the diffusion process?

options.text: It adds more noise to the image
It expands the final latent code into a full-resolution image
It compresses the image even smaller

exercises.explain: The decoder's job is to take the small, cleaned-up latent code and expand it into the big, detailed final picture.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The latent code itself looks just like the final picture, only smaller.
exercises.explain: False! The latent code is a compressed set of numbers that doesn't visually resemble the final image at all — it needs the decoder to become a real picture.

exercises.sentence: After reverse diffusion finishes, a ___ expands the latent code into a full-resolution image.
exercises.explain: The decoder is the network responsible for turning the compact latent code into the final, full-size image.

exercises.prompt: Why can't we just look directly at the latent code to see the final picture?

options.text: Because the latent code is a compressed set of numbers, not a viewable image
Because the latent code is stored on a different computer
Because the latent code is always black and white

exercises.explain: The latent code is compact and encoded — it must be decoded/expanded first before it becomes something we can actually see as a picture.

exercises.prompt: An AI art generator finishes cleaning up noise in latent space. What MUST happen next before you can see the final image?

options.text: The latent code must be decoded/expanded into a full-resolution image
The latent code must be sent back through the noise-adding process
Nothing — the latent code is already the final image
The latent code must be deleted and a new one created

exercises.explain: Correct! The decoder takes the finished latent code and expands it into the full-resolution image so you can actually see the result.
